key_insight: A machine-learning potential shows that Mg-related transmutation defects and irradiation defects in SiC can reduce thermal conductivity in strongly configuration-dependent ways, with V_C and Mg_TC acting as the most effective phonon scatterers.
engineering_relevance: This provides a practical atomistic tool for predicting heat-transport degradation in reactor-grade SiC and for assessing how defect clustering changes thermal performance in nuclear environments.
method: Trained MLIP4SiC-Mg on a large DFT dataset for 3C-SiC with intrinsic point defects, Mg defects, and Mg-defect complexes.
Combined Green-Kubo molecular dynamics with force-error correction to compute thermal conductivity in large defective supercells.
Used a resistance-based dilute-defect treatment to quantify residual thermal resistivity and defect-scattering strength.
evidence_signal: The corrected thermal conductivity of pristine 3C-SiC is 421 W/(mK) at 300 K, matching experiment, and the model reproduces DFT energies, forces, phonons, and thermal conductivities closely.
watch_item: Defect resistivity is not strictly linear with concentration, so dilute-defect extrapolations and clustered-defect interpretations require caution.
paper_title: Thermal Transport in SiC with Intrinsic Defects and Mg Transmutation Products
